_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Blue Earth County, MN. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses — Mankato as US-Dakota War epicenter, mass execution site, treaty-era records.
display_name: Blue Earth County, Minnesota — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: County offices moved through several buildings during the first decade. Records were kept in various locations, occasionally in private offices.

previous_courthouses.location: Mankato, first permanent courthouse on the bluff
previous_courthouses.fate: Outgrown and replaced by the current 1889 Romanesque building. Records transferred.

records_destroyed: scattered loose papers from justice of the peace courts
some township records from outlying areas
record_losses.description: During the US-Dakota War of August–December 1862, Mankato became a military headquarters and refugee center. The town's public buildings were commandeered for military use, and the confusion of the crisis resulted in the loss of some loose records from outlying townships. The bound county records — deeds, probate, court minutes — survived intact. However, the execution of 38 Dakota men at Mankato on December 26, 1862, generated military records that are held at the National Archives, not in the county courthouse.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Blue Earth County's core records survived the war, but the crisis of 1862 created gaps in the peripheral documentation — justice of the peace records, township minutes — that are unlikely to be recovered. If {ancestor_name} was in Blue Earth County during the war, the military records at the National Archives may supplement the county records.
record_losses.source: Hughes, Thomas. History of Blue Earth County and Biographies of its Leading Citizens. Chicago: Middle West Publishing, 1909.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from county organization. The Treaty of Traverse des Sioux (1851) opened the land to settlement, and the earliest deeds reflect the rush of speculation that followed.
deeds.gaps: No known major gaps. Some early entries reference pre-county private land claims that are documented only in federal land office records.
deeds.repository: Blue Earth County Recorder, Mankato; Minnesota Historical Society microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com MN land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — probate records survive from earliest settlement
wills_estates.gaps: Early estates may have been settled informally, especially among immigrant families unfamiliar with American probate procedures.
wills_estates.repository: Blue Earth County Probate Court, Mankato; Minnesota Historical Society

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from county organization
marriages.gaps: Some early marriages were performed by justices of the peace whose records are incomplete. Church marriages, especially Norwegian Lutheran and German Lutheran, were not always recorded civilly before 1870.
marriages.repository: Blue Earth County Recorder; Minnesota Historical Society microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — district court minutes survive from organization
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records are fragmentary, especially for the 1862 period when outlying courts were disrupted.
court_minutes.repository: Blue Earth County Courthouse; Minnesota Historical Society

vital_records.completeness: Compliance was slow in the 1870s and 1880s. Mankato city records are generally more complete than rural township records. The county's mixed ethnic population (Yankee, German, Norwegian, Swedish) means church records in multiple languages supplement civil records.
vital_records.repository: Minnesota Department of Health; Blue Earth County Recorder; Minnesota Historical Society

naturalization_records.completeness: Good — naturalization records from statehood. The county's diverse immigrant population generated substantial naturalization filings.
naturalization_records.gaps: Some immigrants filed initial papers in transit counties (Winona, Hennepin) before reaching Blue Earth County.
naturalization_records.repository: Blue Earth County District Court; National Archives — Chicago; Minnesota Historical Society

substitute_sources.source_type: Lutheran and German Catholic church records
substitute_sources.description: Norwegian Synod, Hauge Synod, German Lutheran (Missouri and Wisconsin Synods), and German Catholic congregations all maintained detailed records. Mankato's First Lutheran Church (Norwegian) and Sts. Peter and Paul Catholic Church (German) have records from the 1850s.
substitute_sources.repository: Norwegian-American Historical Association, Northfield; Luther Seminary Archives, St. Paul; Concordia Historical Institute, St. Louis (Missouri Synod); individual parishes; FamilySearch microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records are the primary substitute for civil vital records before 1870. Norwegian ministerialbok entries often include the immigrant's parish of origin. German Catholic records may be in Latin or German.
substitute_sources.source: Nelson, O. N. History of the Scandinavians and Successful Scandinavians in the United States. Minneapolis, 1904.

substitute_sources.description: Federal population schedules 1850–1940 and Minnesota state/territorial censuses. The 1857 territorial census captures the boom-period settlers.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Minnesota Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Blue Earth County's mixed ethnic population makes census records especially valuable for tracking family origins. The 1860 census captures the pre-war population; the 1870 census reveals who returned and who did not.
substitute_sources.source: Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Census Records Guide. St. Paul: MHS, 2004.

substitute_sources.source_type: US-Dakota War military and prisoner records
substitute_sources.description: Military records from the US-Dakota War, including the military commission trial transcripts and the execution records from Mankato (December 26, 1862), are held at the National Archives. Settler refugee claims and damage assessments are at the Minnesota Historical Society.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives, Washington, DC; Minnesota Historical Society; Blue Earth County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: If {ancestor_name} served in the militia during the war, filed a damage claim, or was among the refugees, these records provide detailed documentation of the family's experience during the crisis.
substitute_sources.source: Chomsky, Carol. The United States-Dakota War Trials: A Study in Military Injustice. Stanford Law Review, vol. 43, no. 1 (1990).

narrative_hooks: Blue Earth County's deed books run unbroken from 1853, which means the land that {ancestor_name} bought or sold in this county can be traced through a continuous chain of title — from the Treaty of Traverse des Sioux to the present.

If {ancestor_name} was in Blue Earth County during the US-Dakota War, the military records at the National Archives and the damage claims at the Minnesota Historical Society may tell the story the county records do not — the militia service, the refugee flight, the claims filed for lost property.

The Norwegian and German church records in Blue Earth County are essential because Minnesota's vital registration law of 1870 was only weakly enforced in rural areas — the pastor's register may be the only record of {ancestor_name}'s birth, marriage, or burial.

The 1889 Blue Earth County Courthouse still stands in Mankato, and the records inside it span the county's entire history — from the speculative boom of the 1850s through the war crisis of 1862 to the agricultural transformation of the early 1900s.
source_references: Hughes, Thomas. History of Blue Earth County and Biographies of its Leading Citizens. Chicago: Middle West Publishing, 1909.
Nelson, O. N. History of the Scandinavians and Successful Scandinavians in the United States. Minneapolis, 1904.
Chomsky, Carol. The United States-Dakota War Trials: A Study in Military Injustice. Stanford Law Review, vol. 43, no. 1 (1990).
Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Census Records Guide. St. Paul: MHS, 2004.
